summary: This PR adds header injection protection by checking header values for return/newline characters in `prepare_headers`. The approach has several correctness and security gaps: validation is not applied to header names, headers added after `prepare_headers` (auth, cookies, body) bypass the check entirely, and the regex itself has a subtle `$` vs `\Z` anchoring bug. Code style in both the models and tests also needs cleanup.

findings.title: Regex validates header values only — header names are not checked
findings.detail: The injection-prevention pattern `pat` is applied only to `val` (the header value) but never to `name` (the header name). An attacker who can supply a crafted header name containing `\r\n` — e.g. `{'foo\r\nX-Injected: evil': 'bar'}` — can still inject arbitrary headers or split the HTTP request. After the `to_native_string` conversion, `name` must also be matched against the pattern, and the exception message should reference whichever of `name`/`val` failed.

findings.title: Validation bypassed for headers added after `prepare_headers`
findings.detail: The CRLF check only runs inside `prepare_headers`. Headers added later in the request lifecycle — `Content-Length`/`Transfer-Encoding` set in `prepare_body`, `Cookie` set in `prepare_cookies`, `Authorization`/`Proxy-Authorization` set during `prepare_auth` and redirect proxy rebuilding — are never validated. Any of those paths that incorporate externally-controlled data (e.g. credentials from a URL, netrc, or proxy environment variables) would bypass sanitisation entirely. The validation should be applied at the point of insertion (e.g. via `CaseInsensitiveDict.__setitem__`) or repeated at each of those sites.

findings.title: Regex `$` anchor permits trailing `\n` — use `\Z` instead
findings.detail:
Python's `re` module treats `$` as matching just before a trailing `
` at the end of the string (even without `re.MULTILINE`). As a result, `re.match(r'^\S[^
]*$', 'foo
')` unexpectedly matches because `$` anchors before the trailing `
` rather than after it. A header value of `'foo
'` will therefore pass validation and not raise `InvalidHeader`. Replace `$` with `\Z` (absolute end-of-string anchor) to close this bypass.

findings.title: No test for invalid header *names* (keys containing `\r`/`\n`)
findings.detail: The validation loop only checks header values; there is no test exercising a header key/name that contains `\r`, `\n`, or leading whitespace. If validation of names is intentionally omitted, this is an untested security boundary; if validation is expected (as the related finding recommends), both the logic and the test are absent.

findings.title: Regex recompiled on every call to `prepare_headers`
findings.detail: The pattern is compiled fresh on each invocation of `prepare_headers`. Although Python's `re` module maintains a small internal cache, relying on it is fragile. Consistent with the existing module-level constants in this file (e.g. `DEFAULT_REDIRECT_LIMIT`, `REDIRECT_STATI`), the compiled regex should be hoisted to a module-level constant such as `VALID_HEADER_REGEX = re.compile(r'^\S[^\r\n]*\Z|^\Z')`.

findings.title: Missing blank line between class definitions and trailing spaces in docstrings
findings.detail: The existing style in `exceptions.py` consistently places a blank line between class definitions. The new `InvalidHeader` class is missing the blank line separating it from `InvalidURL` above and `ChunkedEncodingError` below. Additionally, the docstrings on both `InvalidURL` (line 83) and `InvalidHeader` (line 86) contain a trailing space before the closing `"""`, inconsistent with other one-liner docstrings in the file such as `"""An HTTP error occurred."""`; these trailing spaces should be removed.

findings.title: Incomplete test coverage: only one valid header asserted; no multi-header invalid-entry test
findings.detail: (1) `test_header_validation` defines `headers_ok` with three entries (`foo`, `bar`, `baz`) but only asserts the value of `foo`. Regressions that incorrectly reject empty-string or single-character values would go undetected. (2) All invalid-header tests pass a single-entry dict; there is no test with a dict where the first header is valid and a later one is invalid, to confirm the loop raises on the offending entry rather than silently skipping it.

findings.title: Regex does not block lone vertical-tab (`\x0b`) or form-feed (`\x0c`) characters
findings.detail: RFC 7230 forbids any bare control character in header field values. The pattern `[^\r\n]` only excludes carriage-return and line-feed; characters such as `\x0b` (vertical tab) and `\x0c` (form feed) are also illegal and may be treated as line separators by some server implementations. Consider a broader exclusion such as `[^\x00-\x1f\x7f]` matching the RFC 7230 `field-vchar` production, or at minimum adding `\x0b\x0c` to the excluded set.

findings.title: `to_native_string` on bytes header values may raise `UnicodeDecodeError` instead of `InvalidHeader`
findings.detail: The PR now calls `to_native_string(val)` on header values. In Python 3, `to_native_string` calls `.decode('ascii')` on `bytes` inputs. If a header value is `bytes` containing non-ASCII data, this raises a `UnicodeDecodeError` rather than the expected `InvalidHeader`, which is a confusing API regression. Previously the value was left as-is and passed to urllib3 for handling. The error should be caught and re-raised as `InvalidHeader`.

findings.title: Error message interpolates raw, unsanitised header name
findings.detail: The `InvalidHeader` message at line 415 interpolates the user-supplied `name` directly: `'Invalid return character or leading space in header: %s' % (name)`. If `name` contains CRLF or other control characters (which are not yet validated), the resulting string can corrupt log output or terminal display. Use `repr(name)` (or equivalent) when building the message.

findings.title: No direct unit test for `prepare_headers(None)` / `prepare_headers({})`
findings.detail: The refactored `prepare_headers` always initialises `self.headers = CaseInsensitiveDict()` before the `if headers:` branch. All existing tests call `prepare_headers` indirectly via `requests.get(...)` with a non-None dict. A direct unit-level test calling `prepare_headers(None)` and `prepare_headers({})` is missing to confirm the header dict is properly initialised and no exception is raised.

findings.title: No test for non-string (e.g. integer) header values passed to `prepare_headers`
findings.detail: There is no test covering what happens when a non-string header value such as `{'foo': 123}` is provided. It is unclear whether `to_native_string` handles integers correctly and whether validation still passes or raises an unexpected error. A test case should document the expected behaviour.

findings.title: Missing space after comma in test method signature
findings.detail: `def test_header_validation(self,httpbin):` is missing a space after the comma. All existing test methods in this file use `def test_foo(self, httpbin):` style.

findings.title: Missing space after `#` in inline comments within tests
findings.detail: Comments such as `#test for newline`, `#test for line feed`, `#test for carriage return`, `#test for whitespace`, and `#test for tab` are missing the conventional space after `#`. The existing test file uses `# comment` style throughout.

findings.title: Missing space after `#` in inline comment
findings.detail: The comment `#Regex checks that string doesn't begin with whitespace...` is missing the conventional space after `#`. All existing comments in this file use the `# comment text` style (e.g. line 38). All new comments in this block should follow the same convention.

findings.title: Unused variable `r` inside `pytest.raises` blocks
findings.detail: Each `with pytest.raises(InvalidHeader):` block assigns the result of `requests.get(...)` to `r`, which is never used. Existing tests in this file omit capturing the return value when it is not needed. The assignments should be dropped.

findings.title: Tab character not actually tested — misleading variable name and comment
findings.detail: `headers_tab` is assigned `{'foo': '   bar'}` (three spaces) and the comment reads `#test for tab`, but no actual tab character (`\t`) appears in the test. RFC 7230 treats horizontal tab as obsolete leading whitespace; a test with a real `\t` leading character is missing. Either add a genuine `\t` test case or rename the variable/comment to reflect that it tests spaces.

findings.title: Non-descriptive variable name `pat` for compiled regex
findings.detail: The name `pat` is vague. If the regex is promoted to a module-level constant (see related finding), a descriptive name like `VALID_HEADER_REGEX` is preferable. Even as a local variable, something like `header_val_re` would be more self-documenting and consistent with the naming style of the surrounding code.

findings.title: Regex only validates header values, not header names
findings.detail: The pattern `pat` is only checked against `val` (the header value), but header names can also be injection vectors. A header name containing `\r\n` could be used to inject arbitrary headers. The check should also be applied to `name` after the `to_native_string` conversion.

findings.title: Validation only runs when `headers` is truthy — headers added later bypass the check
findings.detail: The validation is inside `if headers:`, meaning it only applies to headers passed into `prepare_headers`. Headers that are added or mutated on `self.headers` after this call (e.g., `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Authorization` added in `prepare_body`, `prepare_auth`, cookie headers, etc.) are never validated. An attacker who can influence those values would bypass the check entirely.

findings.title: Regex compiled on every call to `prepare_headers`
findings.detail: The pattern `re.compile(r'^\S[^\r\n]*$|^$')` is recompiled on every call to `prepare_headers`. It should be a module-level constant (e.g. `_CLEAN_HEADER_REGEX_STR = re.compile(...)`) so it is only compiled once. While Python's re module caches a small number of recently used patterns, relying on that cache is fragile and can cause subtle performance issues if the cache is evicted.

findings.title: Regex allows a single non-whitespace character followed by `\r` or `\n` at end of string
`to_native_string` on header value may raise for non-ASCII values without clear error
findings.detail: The original code only called `to_native_string` on the header *name*. This PR now also calls `to_native_string(val)` on header values. For Python 3, `to_native_string` calls `.decode('ascii')` on bytes values. If a header value is `bytes` containing non-ASCII data, this will raise a `UnicodeDecodeError` rather than an `InvalidHeader`, which is a potentially confusing API regression. The prior code left the value as-is and let urllib3 handle it.

findings.title: Header injection check does not validate header names
findings.detail: The regex validation at line 411 is only applied to header values (`val`), not to header names (`name`). An attacker who can supply a crafted header name containing `\r\n` (e.g. `{'foo\r\nX-Injected: evil': 'bar'}`) could still inject arbitrary headers or split the HTTP request. Both `name` and `val` should be validated against the injection-prevention pattern.

findings.title: Validation bypassed for headers added after prepare_headers (auth, cookies, body, redirect path)
findings.detail: The CRLF check only runs inside `prepare_headers`. Headers added later — e.g. `Content-Length` / `Transfer-Encoding` set in `prepare_body` (lines 454-456), `Cookie` set in `prepare_cookies` (line 531), `Authorization` and `Proxy-Authorization` set during `prepare_auth` / `rebuild_proxies` in session redirect handling — bypass this check entirely. Any of those paths that incorporate externally-controlled data (e.g., credentials from a URL or netrc, proxy usernames from the environment) would not be sanitised. The validation should also be applied (or enforced at write time via `CaseInsensitiveDict.__setitem__`) to cover these code paths.

findings.title: Regex does not block lone vertical-tab (\x0b) or form-feed (\x0c) characters in header values
findings.detail: RFC 7230 forbids any bare control character in header field values. The regex `r'^\S[^\r\n]*$|^$'` only excludes `\r` and `\n`. Characters such as `\x0b` (vertical tab) and `\x0c` (form feed) are also illegal in header values per the HTTP spec and could be used for injection on some server implementations that treat them as line separators. Consider using a broader exclusion such as `[^\x00-\x1f\x7f]` (matching the RFC 7230 `field-vchar` production) or at minimum adding `\x0b\x0c` to the excluded set.

findings.title: Error message leaks the header name without sanitisation
findings.detail: The `InvalidHeader` exception message at line 415 interpolates the raw, user-supplied header `name` directly: `'Invalid return character or leading space in header: %s' % (name)`. If the header name itself contains CRLF or other control characters (which are not validated), the resulting string could corrupt log output or exception displays. The name should be sanitised (e.g. `repr(name)`) before being included in the error message.

findings.title: Missing blank line between class definitions
findings.detail: The existing style in this file consistently places a blank line between each class definition (e.g., between `InvalidSchema` and `ChunkedEncodingError`). After removing the blank line from `InvalidURL`'s docstring change, `InvalidHeader` (line 85) is missing the blank line that should separate it from both `InvalidURL` above and `ChunkedEncodingError` below. The blank line before `ChunkedEncodingError` (line 88) is also absent.

findings.title: Trailing space in `InvalidURL` docstring
findings.detail: The docstring on line 83 reads `"""The URL provided was somehow invalid. """` with a trailing space before the closing `"""`. The existing one-liner docstrings in this file (e.g., `"""An HTTP error occurred."""`) do not have a trailing space. The new `InvalidHeader` docstring on line 86 has the same issue.

findings.title: Missing space after `#` in inline comment
findings.detail: The comment `#Regex checks that string doesn't begin with whitespace...` is missing the conventional space after `#`. The existing codebase uses `# comment text` style (e.g., line 38: `#: The set of HTTP status codes...`). All new comments in this block should follow the same convention.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside method on every call
findings.detail: The pattern `pat = re.compile(r'^\ S[^\r\n]*$|^$')` is compiled fresh on every invocation of `prepare_headers`. The existing module-level constants pattern in this file (e.g., `DEFAULT_REDIRECT_LIMIT`, `REDIRECT_STATI`) suggests module-level constants are preferred. This regex would be better placed as a module-level compiled constant such as `VALID_HEADER_VAL = re.compile(r'^\S[^\r\n]*$|^$')`.

findings.title: Non-descriptive variable name `pat` for compiled regex
findings.detail: The name `pat` is vague. The surrounding code tends to use more descriptive names. Something like `header_val_re` or `VALID_HEADER_VALUE` (if promoted to module-level) would be more self-documenting and consistent with the style of named constants elsewhere in the file.

findings.title: Missing space after comma in method signature
findings.detail: `def test_header_validation(self,httpbin):` is missing a space after the comma. The existing test methods all use `def test_foo(self, httpbin):` style.

findings.title: Missing space after `#` in inline comments within tests
findings.detail: Comments like `#test for newline`, `#test for line feed`, `#test for carriage return`, `#test for whitespace`, and `#test for tab` are all missing the conventional space after `#`. The existing test file uses `# comment` style (e.g., line 36: `# Requests to this URL should always fail...`).

findings.title: Unused variable `r` in `pytest.raises` blocks
findings.detail: Inside each `with pytest.raises(InvalidHeader):` block, the result is assigned to `r` (e.g., `r = requests.get(...)`), but `r` is never used. Looking at the existing tests, `with pytest.raises(...)` blocks don't bother capturing the return value when it isn't needed (e.g., lines 75-77). The assignments should be dropped.

findings.title: Tab described as whitespace in `test_header_no_leading_space`
findings.detail: `headers_tab` is assigned `{'foo': '   bar'}` — this looks like three spaces, not a tab character. If the intent is to test a tab (`\t`), the value should be `'\tbar'`. If spaces are intended, the variable name `headers_tab` is misleading.

findings.title: No test for invalid header names (keys with return/whitespace characters)
findings.detail: The validation loop in `prepare_headers` only checks the header value (`val`) but not the header name (`name`). There is no test exercising the case where the header *key/name* itself contains `\r`, `\n`, or leading whitespace. If validation of names is intentionally omitted that is still an untested code path boundary; if validation of names is expected it is both a logic gap and an untested gap.

findings.title: No test for `prepare_headers` called directly with `None` / no headers
findings.detail: The refactored `prepare_headers` now always initialises `self.headers = CaseInsensitiveDict()` before the `if headers:` branch. The existing tests only call `prepare_headers` indirectly via `requests.get(…)` with non-None headers dicts. There is no unit-level test that calls `prepare_headers(None)` or `prepare_headers({})` directly to confirm the header dict is properly initialised and no exception is raised in the empty/None case.

findings.title: No test for header value that is a non-string type (e.g. integer) passed to `prepare_headers`
findings.detail: `to_native_string(val)` is called before the regex match. There is no test covering what happens when a non-string header value (e.g. `{'foo': 123}`) is provided — whether `to_native_string` converts it correctly and validation still passes, or whether it raises an unexpected error.

findings.title: `test_header_validation` only asserts one of the three accepted header values
findings.detail: The test defines `headers_ok` with three entries (`foo`, `bar`, `baz`) but only asserts the value of `headers_ok['foo']`. The `bar` (numeric-string) and `baz` (empty string) cases are silently accepted without assertion, so a regression that rejects empty-string or single-character values would go undetected.

findings.title: `test_header_no_leading_space`: tab character (`\t`) not tested, only spaces
findings.detail: The test uses `'   bar'` (three spaces) as the second case and labels it 'tab' in the comment, but there is no actual tab character (`\t`). RFC 7230 treats horizontal tab as obsolete whitespace too; a test with a real `\t` leading character is missing.

findings.title: No test for multi-header dict where only the second (or later) header is invalid
findings.detail: All existing invalid-header tests pass a single-entry dict. There is no test that passes a dict where the first header is valid and a later one is invalid, to confirm the loop raises on the offending entry rather than silently skipping it.

findings.title: Regex validates header values only — header names are not checked
findings.detail: The injection-prevention pattern `pat` is applied only to `val` (the header value) but never to `name` (the header name). An attacker who can supply a crafted header name containing `\r\n` — e.g. `{'foo\r\nX-Injected: evil': 'bar'}` — can still inject arbitrary headers or split the HTTP request. After the `to_native_string` conversion, `name` must also be matched against the pattern, and the exception message should reference whichever of `name`/`val` failed.

findings.title: Validation bypassed for headers added after `prepare_headers`
findings.detail: The CRLF check only runs inside `prepare_headers`. Headers added later in the request lifecycle — `Content-Length`/`Transfer-Encoding` set in `prepare_body`, `Cookie` set in `prepare_cookies`, `Authorization`/`Proxy-Authorization` set during `prepare_auth` and redirect proxy rebuilding — are never validated. Any of those paths that incorporate externally-controlled data (e.g. credentials from a URL, netrc, or proxy environment variables) would bypass sanitisation entirely. The validation should be applied at the point of insertion (e.g. via `CaseInsensitiveDict.__setitem__`) or repeated at each of those sites.

findings.detail:
Python's `re` module treats `$` as matching just before a trailing `
` at the end of the string (even without `re.MULTILINE`). As a result, `re.match(r'^\S[^
]*$', 'foo
')` unexpectedly matches because `$` anchors before the trailing `
` rather than after it. A header value of `'foo

findings.detail: The validation loop only checks header values; there is no test exercising a header key/name that contains `\r`, `\n`, or leading whitespace. If validation of names is intentionally omitted, this is an untested security boundary; if validation is expected (as the related finding recommends), both the logic and the test are absent.

findings.detail: The pattern is compiled fresh on each invocation of `prepare_headers`. Although Python's `re` module maintains a small internal cache, relying on it is fragile. Consistent with the existing module-level constants in this file (e.g. `DEFAULT_REDIRECT_LIMIT`, `REDIRECT_STATI`), the compiled regex should be hoisted to a module-level constant such as `VALID_HEADER_REGEX = re.compile(r'^\S[^\r\n]*\Z|^\Z')`.

findings.title: Missing blank line between class definitions and trailing spaces in docstrings
findings.detail: The existing style in `exceptions.py` consistently places a blank line between class definitions. The new `InvalidHeader` class is missing the blank line separating it from `InvalidURL` above and `ChunkedEncodingError` below. Additionally, the docstrings on both `InvalidURL` (line 83) and `InvalidHeader` (line 86) contain a trailing space before the closing `"""`, inconsistent with other one-liner docstrings in the file such as `"""An HTTP error occurred."""`; these trailing spaces should be removed.

findings.detail: (1) `test_header_validation` defines `headers_ok` with three entries (`foo`, `bar`, `baz`) but only asserts the value of `foo`.
